banner: Week 39 · Capstone Build — Plan Your Year in One Project
essential_question: If you had to prove, in one project, that you learned geometry this year — what would you build?
objective: Design a culminating capstone that combines a real-world trig height/distance measurement, a 3D-printed (or built) solid with computed volume/surface area, and a coordinate-plane transformation design — and plan how each piece will be measured and proven.

content.prompt: Look back at Weeks 12, 22, and 35 — what did each Unit Studio teach you to build? Your year-end capstone borrows one piece from each: a trig measurement, a solid, and a transformation.

content.heading: One capstone, three proofs
bullets: Part 1 — a real height or distance found by an angle of elevation/depression and right-triangle trig.
Part 2 — a 3D-printed or built solid with computed volume and surface area, verified by measurement.
Part 3 — a coordinate-plane transformation (or sequence of transformations) of a figure, with a short proof that it's rigid (or, if a dilation, a similarity).

content.heading: Brainstorm before you commit
content.note: Brainstorm real objects to measure (a tree, a roofline, a flagpole, a distance across the yard). Brainstorm a solid to build or print — tie it to something real if you can. Brainstorm a figure and a transformation (or sequence) you'll perform on it.

content.heading: Match the tool to the task

content.preview: Next session: Build Day — make all three parts real and record every measurement.

success_criteria.text: I can describe a real-world object and angle setup I will use for a trig height/distance measurement
I can describe a solid I will build/print and the formulas I will use to verify its volume and surface area
I can describe a figure and a transformation (or sequence) I will perform on the coordinate plane, and what I'll need to prove about it

questions.prompt: Plan Part 1 (Trig): name the real object or distance you'll measure, and how you'll find the angle (protractor, inclinometer app, etc.).
questions.answer: Open-ended, e.g.: 'Measure the height of the backyard tree using an angle of elevation from 20 ft away, read with a protractor/string plumb-bob.'

questions.prompt: Plan Part 2 (Solid): name the solid you'll build or 3D-print, its approximate dimensions, and which volume/surface-area formulas you'll need.
questions.answer: Open-ended, e.g.: 'A cylinder, radius 2 in, height 5 in — V = πr²h and total surface area = 2πr² + 2πrh.'

questions.prompt: Plan Part 3 (Transformation): name the figure you'll place on the coordinate plane and the transformation (or sequence) you'll perform, plus what you'll need to prove about the result (e.g. that side lengths stayed the same).
questions.answer: Open-ended, e.g.: 'A triangle with vertices (1,1), (4,1), (1,5), rotated 90° about the origin — I'll need to prove side lengths and angles are unchanged.'

questions.prompt: Which formula will you need to verify a 3D-printed cone's volume?
questions.explain: A cone's volume is one-third of a cylinder with the same base and height.

questions.prompt: Exit: what's the ONE part of this three-part capstone you're most unsure how to pull off — and what's your first step to figure it out before Build Day?
